entries.name: bass clef
entries.means: The fancy F at the start of a staff. Its two dots surround the line where F sits — that's how you anchor every other note. Bass music lives in this clef. (Bass parts are written one octave higher than they sound, so they fit on the staff.)

entries.name: treble clef
entries.means: The swirly symbol used for higher instruments (guitar, vocals, piano right hand). You'll see it on other people's charts — bassists read the bass clef.

entries.name: staff
entries.means: The five horizontal lines music is written on. Higher on the staff = higher pitch. Notes sit on lines or in the spaces between them.

entries.name: ledger line
entries.means: A short extra line for notes that spill above or below the staff. The bass's open E sits one ledger line below the bass staff.

entries.name: bar line
entries.means: A vertical line that marks off one bar (one group of beats). A double bar with a thick line means the end.

entries.name: flat
entries.means: Lower the note by one semitone — one fret down toward the headstock. B♭ is one fret below B.

entries.name: sharp
entries.means: Raise the note by one semitone — one fret up toward the body. F♯ is one fret above F.

entries.name: natural
entries.means: Cancel a sharp or flat — play the plain letter-name note.

entries.name: key signature
entries.means: The sharps or flats clustered right after the clef: they apply to those notes for the entire piece. One sharp = the key of G major (or E minor); two flats = B♭ major (or G minor).

entries.name: whole note
entries.means: A note held for four beats — a whole bar of 4/4. Written as an empty oval with no stem.

entries.name: half note
entries.means: Two beats. An empty oval with a stem.

entries.name: quarter note
entries.means: One beat — the foot-tap. A filled-in oval with a stem. Four of them fill a bar of 4/4.

entries.name: eighth note
entries.means: Half a beat. A filled oval with a stem and one flag; groups get beamed together. Count them "1 & 2 &…".

entries.name: sixteenth note
entries.means: A quarter of a beat — funk lives here. Two flags or a double beam. Count "1 e & a 2 e & a…".

entries.name: triplet
entries.means: A small 3 over a beamed group: squeeze three equal notes into the space of two. The engine of shuffle and 6/8 feels.

entries.name: whole rest
entries.means: Four beats of silence — a hanging box under a staff line. (An entire empty bar also uses this symbol in any meter.)

entries.name: half rest
entries.means: Two beats of silence — a box sitting on a staff line (a hat, not a hole).

entries.name: quarter rest
entries.means: One beat of deliberate silence. Rests are played — muting on time is a core bass skill.

entries.name: eighth rest
entries.means: Half a beat of silence — a little flag on a stick. Funk bass is written with as many of these as notes.

entries.name: dot (after a note)
entries.means: Adds half the note's own length. A dotted quarter = one and a half beats.

entries.name: tie
entries.means: A curve joining two notes of the SAME pitch: pluck once, hold for the combined length. Ties carry notes across beats and bar lines.

entries.name: four-four time
entries.means: Four quarter-note beats per bar — the time signature of most rock, pop, and funk. So common it's also written 𝄴 ("common time").

entries.name: three-four time
entries.means: Three quarter-note beats per bar — waltz time. Count "ONE two three".

entries.name: six-eight time
entries.means: Six eighth notes per bar felt as TWO big rolling beats of three — the lilt of slow blues and Irish jigs alike. Count "ONE-two-three FOUR-five-six".

entries.name: tempo marking
entries.means: The metronome speed: 92 quarter-note beats per minute. Practice exercises on this site give these as start and target tempos.

entries.name: fermata
entries.means: The eye over a note: hold it longer than written, until the leader cuts it off. Watch the drummer.

entries.name: soft & loud (piano, forte)
entries.means: Volume marks: p = soft, f = loud, with mp/mf between and pp/ff at the extremes. On bass this is pluck strength — and it's half of what people call feel.

entries.name: crescendo / decrescendo
entries.means: Hairpins: gradually get louder (<) or softer (>) across the marked stretch.

entries.name: accent
entries.means: Hit this one note harder so it pokes out of the line. Where the accents go defines the groove as much as the notes do.

entries.name: staccato dot
entries.means: A dot above/below the notehead: cut the note short and leave air after it. Note length is a bassist's superpower.

entries.name: slur (legato)
entries.means: A curve over DIFFERENT pitches: connect them smoothly with no gap — on bass usually hammer-ons, pull-offs, or slides.

entries.name: ghost note
entries.means: An × notehead: a muted, pitchless "thunk" — rhythm without a clear note. The percussion inside funk and R&B bass lines.

entries.name: repeat signs
entries.means: Thick double bars with two dots: play the section between them again. With no opening sign, repeat from the top.

entries.name: first & second endings
entries.means: Bracketed bars at a repeat: play ending 1 the first time through, skip to ending 2 on the repeat.

entries.name: segno
entries.means: The fancy S with a slash: a bookmark. "D.S." (dal segno) means jump back to this sign.

entries.name: coda
entries.means: The crosshair circle: the tail section of a song. "To Coda ⊕" means jump from here to the section marked with the same symbol.

entries.name: da capo / dal segno
entries.means: Road-map jumps: D.C. = go back to the top; D.S. = go back to the segno sign. "al Fine" = play until the word Fine; "al Coda" = until the coda jump.

entries.name: simile / repeat-bar sign
entries.means: A slash with dots inside a bar: play the same thing as the previous bar. Charts use it to avoid rewriting a groove 24 times.

entries.name: major chord
entries.means: A letter alone means a major triad. As the bassist: its root (C) is your first-choice note.

entries.name: minor chord
entries.means: m (or −, or "min") after the letter = minor triad: the third is lowered. Your root doesn't change — but the 3rd you choose in fills does.

entries.name: dominant seventh
entries.means: A plain 7 = major triad + flat seventh — the bluesy, wants-to-move chord. All three chords of a standard blues are this type.

entries.name: major seventh
entries.means: maj7 (or Δ7) = major triad + natural seventh — the dreamy one. Don't confuse with plain C7: the sevenths differ by a fret.

entries.name: minor seventh
entries.means: Minor triad + flat seventh — the mellow workhorse of soul, funk, and jazz. The ii chord in every major key is one of these.

entries.name: half-diminished (m7♭5)
entries.means: Minor seventh chord with its fifth flattened — written ø7 or m7♭5. The tense ii chord of minor keys.

entries.name: diminished
entries.means: The small circle: a stack of minor thirds — maximum tension, usually passing between two stable chords. Bass move: play its root and get out cleanly.

entries.name: suspended chord
entries.means: The third is replaced by the fourth (sus2: by the second) — floating, undecided sound that usually resolves. Root still C; avoid emphasizing thirds in fills.

entries.name: slash chord
entries.means: Chord on the left, YOUR note on the right: C major with E in the bass. A direct instruction to the bassist — play the note after the slash.

entries.name: no chord
entries.means: Everybody stop comping: no harmony here (a break, or riff in unison). Usually the bass either rests or doubles the riff.

entries.name: tablature staff
entries.means: Four lines = the four strings (bottom line = low E string), numbers = which fret to press, 0 = open string. Shows where to play; the notation above it shows what and when.

entries.name: root marker
entries.means: In this site's fretboard diagrams: the filled amber dot marks the root — the home note the shape is built on.

entries.name: finger numbers
entries.means: Fretting-hand fingering: 1 = index, 2 = middle, 3 = ring, 4 = pinky. (Thumb is T, rare on bass fretboards.)

entries.name: hammer-on / pull-off
entries.means: In tab: 5h7 = pluck fret 5, hammer a finger onto 7 without re-plucking; 7p5 = pluck 7, flick the finger off to sound 5.

entries.name: slide
entries.means: In tab: 5/7 = pluck at fret 5 and slide the pressed finger up to 7 (backslash = slide down). One pluck, two notes, smooth sweep between.

entries.name: vibrato
entries.means: Wobble the pitch by rocking the fretting finger — a singer's shimmer on a held note.
